So, we are on page 213. Is where we is. And let's talk first, so let's talk a little bit about ghost writing. I happen to like this work a lot. Over the years I've done a lot of it, continue to do it. I have two projects right now that I'm doing. They are not usually response sensitive. So the stress level is lower because they're either being used for sort of general promotional purposes usually or has part and parcel of a shock and awe package or a series of communications. So in most cases they're not directly getting the response. However, the most important thing to know about all this in selling it to your client is that what we bring to such projects that normal ordinary ghost writers that they might go find in any number of places don't is that their book, their newsletter, their whatever ought to be to some extent a disguised sales letter, not just a book. And so they want a ghost writer who is an advertising slash direct response copywriter. They don't want a ghost writer who is an English professor, academic theorist or professional writer because if they get one of those what they're going to get for example is a book that then one of us has to go fix because it's a great book but it doesn't do what it needs to do from a promotional marketing or sales purpose. So that's our argument for ourselves as ghost writer. The other good news about these kinds of things is when you show them to clients who have a legitimate beneficial reason to have them that can be suggested to them. They want them. There's a certain ego part of all this that comes into play so there's a pleasure reason to buy not just a business reason to buy. We're just about everything else we sell is all about the business. So they actually want to have a book. They want to have an information product for their business. So the guy that owns the gardening center, see he's never thought about having his own course with DVDs and CDs and a book and a package all about how to have the greatest garden ever that he sells or he gives or he's never thought about that. But if you show him a couple such things that's something he can get excited about and he knows his wife and his mama will be proud of. So there's a lot of attachment to this beyond the practical. These are all over the place. So the small business owner can and will only pay so much. It's their transaction size, again matters. So a financial advisor can pay more to get a book done than can somebody run in a pizza kitchen but they can both get a book done. And arguably both they both should get a book done. The national marketer can pay more than the local marketer. In some cases there are a lot of these businesses and niches of co-authored or anthology books where really the main book is just generic and the individual financial advisor gets a chapter, gets the cover made over to him, gets fit in as a co-author. That business exists and it's big and it's profitable and it can be a business you can be in. But this now takes them up a notch but in many niches they are already familiar with those things. They may already have bought them. By the way, most of my clients who've hired me to go to write books in the past five years have already bought one of those. A buyer is a buyer is a buyer is a buyer. They already are in one of those. Now they want their own. So these are really great projects and I encourage you to think about saying to clients, here's something you should do. I gave you a couple of things I want to tell you. I gave you in your bag, you should have had this. You have it for two reasons. One, it's another back office resource for you like the three vendors who were here. Adams are really good guide. They do a nice job. They'll do a turnkey. So you can take your project there and if you want, ISBN number and Amazon distribution and all that for the client, they can arrange that for you. If it doesn't matter, then it doesn't matter. But the other reason I gave it to you is because Adams done a great job of pitching why somebody should have a book. So this book is a sales letter for why anybody in business ought to have their own book. So why reinvent the wheel? Here's your sales points for you. This is, I'll tell you a couple of little things, but I'll show you a couple of things. So this is, this is either the first or the second one I did. This is for a client, Don Dwyer, Don's deceased now. A lot of my clients have in common is they're dead. You live long enough. Well, I mean, look, generally speaking, so especially when I was young, typical clients, 20 years older than I am, because I got to have a lot of money to pay me, right? So they're dying, which is unfortunate because he was a really good client. I bothered some of you, didn't it? You all ought to remember one thing, people, audiences in seminars never think about this, that the person on stage can see them. See, I see your facial expressions, you know, in case you don't know. Now, if there's 600 of you in the room, I don't, but if there's 60, I do. So like, I know what, when you are disapproving, I get it. It doesn't, all it does is encourage me, by the way. So Don's business was, the company is still very profitable, successful. His business, the main business, he's a franchisor. So the main business is rainbow carpet cleaning. So they put people in the carpet cleaning businesses. And so first of all, logic, he was smarter than that, but logic would say we're going to write a book about the carpet cleaning industry or how to be successful in carpet cleaning. But see, that would not elevate him to God's status. If anything, it might diminish him in the way that we engineered this. So we wrote a generic, how to become a successful entrepreneur, regardless of your background. That part important, because most of the people buying carpet cleaning franchises are not PhDs, and they don't have a lot of money. But we wrote a generic success book for him, which has his success story, and some of the stuff he teaches, the carpet cleaners, generic size. So a lot of the raw material did come from him, but then a lot of it didn't. We did a couple of things that are important. We went and I got him a deal with a real publisher. Now again, now these days, Adams company is just as good. So it would have been a lot easier. But that didn't actually exist. So I went and got him a deal with a real book publisher. So he had a real barcode and actually was in bookstores, although that was just the thing that's really just a sales tool, but to make it real. So went to a publisher and said, look, the guy will buy 10,000 books. What skin is it off your nose to put the thing in your catalog? And if a bookstore orders it, fine. But we want a real publisher. It's not an awful book. You can approve the manuscript, but you'll sell 10,000 books. Good, deal done. And Tony had spoken for him. So we got him a four word from Tony, a Robbins. And as I recall, so what year was this originally done? 1993. As I recall, I got 35,000 bucks and 50 cents a book. And I think he burned through about 50,000 copies. So by the time it was done, now the one dumb thing I did that I didn't do is other than an acknowledgement on the page, I didn't make any promotion for me in the book. Now that was stupid because I easily could have done it. There are tons of these guys out there. There's one in your town, at least one. They are the entrepreneurial CEO who has built a business from scratch, right? Who has a story to tell, wants to tell it, has a commercial purpose for having a book because it can promote their business. It can promote them. It can become a part of their marketing packages. It can be either sold or given away to customers. It can be their access to media. So all the 21 reasons are all the logical reasons. So they have logical justification for doing it and spending the money on it. And they can spend company funds, not personal funds. So all of that's right. But they also have emotional reasons why they want to do it. They built their business from scratch. They have a story to tell. They have a philosophy they want to communicate. They're ripe for the picking. Some ways to spot them. If you're trying to spot them from scratch, if the business owner is out speaking, telling his story. So he's speaking at the chamber telling his story. He's speaking at the sales and marketing executives telling his story. If he's getting media, if he's already making a point of trying to get press. And so every once in a while he's showing up in the local newspaper, he's on the local radio station. If he's bought a show on the radio station. So those are great clients, by the way, generally speaking, local clients. They bought the half hour show on Saturday. So the Cairo that's got the health guy that's got the show. The real estate guy that's got the show. The financial guy that's got the show. The auto repair guy that's got the show. Every market's got. I'm in case you're everybody aware of what I'm talking about. Most markets have at least one station. Some have two that sell blocks of time. They usually sell it on weekends when it doesn't disrupt their regular programming. The guy buys the show. Sometimes all the commercials are his. Sometimes he runs around and sells commercials to offset the cost. But he's really doing a radio infomercial. That's what he's doing. Most of them do calling shows. Well, those are great clients. A, they're already promoting. B, they're probably list building. C, they're spending money. And D, they're promoting themselves. They're a guy. A guy for this and a guy for all sorts of other stuff. So there's that book. This is, this is a really good member of ours who's in the pizza business in Canada. So here's our first pizza book. Passion about pizza. Discover the natural health benefits of this ancient food by a Canadian pizza baking champion Diane Kottu. It's a little thin. Doesn't matter. So it's got some chapters about health and fitness. And then it's got a bunch of recipes. So it's got pizza recipes, including recipes for some of the pizza that they sell in the store. Okay. Because nobody's going to make it. They're going to come by it anyway. No harm no foul. But it gets her media. So here she is with her medal that she won from, I don't know, some baking championship somewhere. She's been on a food network. I mean, she's a big promoter and premium price seller, by the way, average price of, average price of a large pizza is 28 bucks in Winnipeg, Canada, not Beverly Hills, California. So she's got like 300 books she's doing. It's a path to media for her. The customers like the fact that they're doing business with a celebrity. So they get to buy the book in a store and give it to mom, give it to their neighbor, you know, whatever. So it's a promotional tool for her. This is, if you subscribe to look over my shoulder and marketing your services, how many of you subscribe to look over your shoulder marketing services? Okay. Those of you who didn't raise your hand, you ought to see the AWI people because like that's sort of, I can't believe you're not doing it. But anyway, if you subscribe, you got this. So this is a, this is really interesting because this is a philosophy book more than it is anything else, but the business is a builder of motorcycles for women. So and this is a useful strategy to teach a business owner is they ought to have a philosophy and they ought to enunciate it and they ought to speak about the emotional aspects of their business regardless of what the product or the service is. And so this is about women motorcycles and mental health. The premise by the way is that you will, there's no, you don't find any motorcycles parked in front of the psychiatrist's office. You only see cars. So if you want to stay sane and women get to exert their personality and, and there's even a chapter about how motorcycles help you have your aha moment. Anybody? You'll get it. You just keep thinking about it. This is, there you go. There's one more. This is, this is, now this is an anthology book. So this is Mike Kapuzi's. And so Mike put this together. Dream Inc. Million Dollar Business Strategies from 32 Successful Philadelphia Area Entrepreneurs. Okay. There's your back. So this is really their stories. If I get this wrong, you yell. I assume that almost everybody paid to play. You got anybody in? You put any celebrities in who didn't pay to play? Couple. So there's a couple like really well-known business owners in Philadelphia who got their chapters for free. Then everybody else paid to play. So everybody gets a book without the expense of doing a book from scratch. He went chapter members mostly I assume. But yes, no. That was the other one. So this is business owners, clients of yours, referrals, you rounded them up, you did it. So there's probably three or four of the 32 that are comps. And the rest of them are paid. So they've paid for some editorial service, the right to be in the book. They had to buy X number of books. So every one of them gets to be in a book without having to do a book from scratch. They get to benefit from promotion to everybody else's in the book because everybody's going to distribute books to their own customers in their own contacts and their own prospects. So if I'm one of the 32, I'm getting marketed to 31 other people's customer bases. So that's an argument for doing it, right? Because there's some internal promotion automatically. Plus I got a good tool. I got the credibility of being in a book with a few famous people. I spent a lot of money, but I assume a nice profitable hit. Yeah, for you. So yeah, I'll entertain one real quick. Did everyone write them a chapter? Yes, no, they got help. We interviewed them. I can play on the call. So probably the process was they interviewed them all. They wrote it for them, went back, tweaked it, then bingo. Okay. Yeah, hit call real quick. Real quick. Got to be real loud, you don't know mine. I got it. Basically my software thought I could build my site. Found me because I was on Amazon searching for a game entity. I didn't know that that was on Amazon. And now when Amazon works, in search of game entity, you find a life of this guy. And now my wife and I have been in business for about two years. Through that book, though. It's awesome. Or he's in your resource directory. So this is a guy that does cartoons for advertising. By the way, good resource for you. The original cartoons you see in some of my books, the Riftless Management and stuff, were done by Vince. So like he gets what we do, so you can give him a marketing concept and he can do a cartoon for it. So this is a book all about why you should use cartoons to sell. So this is his tool. This is his business brochure. This is his business card. This is his sales letter. But it has a lot more impact because it's a book. Now he had him to do his himself, but in most cases, A, it wouldn't occur to him. And B, then he wouldn't do it. So somebody like him is a clock, they're a perfect client. So the other couple things I'll tell you about books. There are companies who are cold prospecting for this, first of all. So here's, this is right out of Robb Report. This is the May issue. Both of those ads there are professional writing, biographies, business books and ghost writing service. Yada, yada, yada. It's a lead generation ad. Star in your own TV show and get your book written. We only accept two clients a month. Da, da, da, da, da, da, da. So they're writers, marketers doing lead generation advertising, sending out a package and signing up a client to ghost write for them. Couple of things that are cool that you can add to this arrangement. One is you can bring some expert or celebrity content to the book you're doing for your client for them. This will never occur to them on their own. They wouldn't have a clue on how to do it if they did, but it's not that difficult. First of all, there's a lot of stuff out there that you can go get and use and all you got to do is give credit. So there's a ton of stuff. Secondly, if you send a letter to, you name it. If you send a letter to Trump and you say we're doing a book on negotiation by Joe Blow negotiation expert and is you guys have a chapter by you or a chapter by George Ross that you'd like to have in the book, you got a reasonably good chance of getting yourself handed a chapter because they're promoters. And if you ask 10, one of them is going to say yes. So you can go round up celebrity or expert content to beef up the guy's book and give him some authority. You can help him find a sponsor in some cases, somebody who doesn't compete with him but who would like to be associated with the book who will pay to have a chapter, to be the sponsor of, to have advertising inserted in the back so you can help offset his cost if you want to. You can bicycle it out once you've done one, now area exclusive all across the country. So if you do a great one for a financial advisor in Bethlehem, Pennsylvania and 90% of the content could be used by any financial advisor and you carefully construct it so 90% of the content could be used by any financial advisor, then all you got to do is take him out and put the other guy in. So now you get an extra run of the book when it gets produced. Now you got your sample and you can go so the guy in Pittsburgh now can be the only one in Pittsburgh to have the book and the only one in LA to have the book and the, well you divide LA up but I mean you get the idea. So you can do the work once and then turn around and resell the work again and again and again and again just tweaking it slightly. You can be in the anthology business as Mike did if you choose to do that. By the way that's also a way to prospect. So anybody in a local area who owns a business that you would select and send them sort of the who's who letter. So we're publishing this book Dream Inc. of the 30 smartest entrepreneurs in Philadelphia and based on your website, your company, the news reporter about your company in the Philadelphia to require you've been selected as a candidate to be included. We need to have a phone conversation. I need to come see you whatever. Well that conversation of course can lead to the bigger, broader conversation of marketing, advertising, etc. for their business. So it's a semi-sneaky way to prospect. Audio video online presentation scripts, much of the same answer applies. First of all, almost every business should be doing multimedia. So they should have audio, they should have video, they should have it online, they should have it in hard presentation form, they should be sending people home with it after they buy in order to make them stick, they should be sending people home with it after they have an appointment. There's all sorts of situations. We did a CD, I did a CD in 1984, 1985 for the chiropractor profession. I did it for one doc first, same model I just described. So I wrote a script for a audio CD called How to Get Well Faster, which really was a reinforcing pitch about chiropractic that was designed to go home with the patient after their report of findings pitch, whether they signed up or not, in part to deal with the spouse and the negative influence of other people. So they could give it to the spouse to listen to, they were supposed to listen to it, it stimulated referrals and so forth. So we did it for this one doc who had a big practice, very aggressive marketer, did a lot of seminars, brought 100 people, 200 people into a seminar, pitched him so everybody got one of these things. So I mean it worked, he was happy, I got a nice, but once I got it all done, well I'll go to all this work to do this for one chiropractor and there's 38,000 of them in America, this doesn't make good sense, right? So now I made it available to all the chiropractors, we were selling other stuff too and I was dealing with through practice management companies and we licensed it. So you want it in your town, you can have it and we'll tweak it, customize it for you, fix the script, you record it, we'll press it, you've got the rights to use it for a year and here's the 52 ways that you should use it too. And again, I don't remember exact numbers now, but I think I sold 400 licenses, 410 licenses, 420 licenses at a thousand bucks a piece, probably spent half the money selling them, so it was $200,000 from, and I think the original fee was like 7500 bucks, so you know which would you rather have, $7500 or $200,000, I'll take the $200,000 any day of the week. So they should have audio and video tools. The case for you being the script writer, which I've given you here on this page, 214, is really the same case as why you should be the ghost writer. The case is you don't want a regular movie writer, TV writer, the guy down at the TV station will do it for free, you don't want any of those people writing this because this has to sell. So like my story about that, I was speaking, I was actually on a panel at DMA and there was some big company that had just decided to do infomercials and they came up and very excitedly told me that they had retained Francis Ford Coppola as an advisor on their infomercials. He did the Godfather movies if you don't know who he is. And I said, I laughed actually, I laughed out loud, and the guy said, what's funny? I said, well, I said, if I'm going to do Godfather 5, that's exactly who I want. I said, if I'm going to do an infomercial to sell auto polish, and you give me a choice between Francis Ford Coppola and an old broken down vacuum cleaner sales guy with one foot bigger than the other from shoving it in the door all his life, and a three piece reversible JC penny suit with cigarette burns all over it, I'm taking the vac guide to write the script any day of the week. Because Coppola don't want to sell anything. The vac guide knows how to sell, and that's what this is about. And I've used that story many, many, many, many times to make that point to clients. So your case for being the script writer is the same. In your sample book, we're going to get to it. I'm going to give you samples of all this stuff, by the way. Basically, you want to take control of these projects. You do not want to write a script and then turn them loose to go film it or go record it. Two reasons. One, that's like writing copy and sending them off to a graphics company and turning them loose. The people you turn them loose to don't know how to sell anything either. So they will ruin what it is that you did one way or the other. Secondly, there's money in it. So when you control the process, you get a little bit of everything because you package. So now you go book the recording studio, you book the engineer, you make arrangements to film the thing, you get the film crew, you make sure you get people that technically know what they're doing. But you are now either marking it up or getting money backwards when you package the whole thing together and you are in a sense the executive producer. I had a really nice arrangement for five years in Phoenix. KPHOTV, which was independent TV station, the show reruns. They were massively overbuilt because at one time they were the leader in live television in Phoenix. Live kids show, live sports show, live cooking show in the morning, all live local TV, which of course is all pretty much died. So this massively overbuilt facility that they didn't use anymore. All still rigged. Cameras, live edit rooms, kitchen set, living room set, news set. I mean, it's gorgeous. As long as I did it off hours at the whole facility, camera men, live edit guy in the switch bay, everything, 5,000 bucks. I'm billing it at 35,000. Boom. I'm knocking out videos like there's no tomorrow. It's great. I mean, my whole worry was they were going to find out what we were packaging this at. I found one in Orlando for somebody a couple of weeks ago that this is beautiful underused facility. They used to do a lot of wrestling videos and they lost the contract and they're available cheap. So if you hunt, right now video production guys are really hurt because they don't know how to sell their service. But you want to control the process. I'm going to give you an audio example where I can told the process. And also keep in mind, if you create something that works for one client who is locally based, there is always the opportunity to turn around and roll it out to like clients in other areas tweaked. So you keep that in mind when you build it. Number one, then you keep it in mind as an opportunity after the fact. So if you create a DVD for a financial advisor in Toledo, Ohio for follow up on prospects that works, there's financial advisors all across the country who can use that DVD. And that often is the bigger opportunity of the two. Speech writing and group presentation script writing. Lots of business owners, small or large, have the opportunity to sell by speech or sell by seminar. And they have no idea how to do it. And if they get somebody to help them with their speech or they do it on their own or they get somebody to help them with their seminar or they do it on their own, guess what it will miss? It'll miss the sales, of course. It'll miss the sales. It will not be structured as a sales presentation. If anything, what they'll do is it will be structured as a speech with a little lousy commercial crammed on the end. It won't be built as a sales presentation from beginning to end. Now here's the good news for all of you. All this is, all these are, are long form sales letters brought to life. That's all they are. And they move back and forth quite easily. So I was the only one who did this, but for nine years when I spoke on the success events, the peer law events. Anybody see me speak at one of these? Any of you guys go to those? Oh, one or two, three. Okay. So for the rest of you quickly, these things are big circuses. So all day long, sports arena, our average attendance was 25,000 people, bunch of speakers, the only two constants for nine years, Zig Ziglar and myself. And then there's like a former U.S. president, a former astronaut, a former Hollywood star, a former this, a former that, interspersed with guys who sell stuff. Some of my famous guys, Tommy Hopkins, Brian Tracy, Jim Marone, me, and unfamous guys. And really it's a giant Tupperware party with 25,000 people to sell stuff. That's what it is. Which is how I always explained it. I followed Norm Schwartzkopf for two years and the first time, because I was on the last. So if the guy in front of me, the famous person in front of me went long, he's costing me money because people are leaving. So I had to try and control that because the promoter was too shy to tell the celebrity and enforce his contract. So I take Norm aside. And Norm says, how does this whole thing work? I said, oh good, I'm glad you asked. Let me explain this to you. I said, here's the way this works. I said, you got a check, 75,000 bucks for showing up. And you can go out there and do anything you want. And they'll be happy, by the way, because they just came to see Norm Schwartzkopf. So you can tell stories, you can give a speech, you can read the L pages backwards. It doesn't make any difference. I said, I'm going to try and get 75,000 dollars, but I got to get it 278 bucks at a time out of their pockets by selling them stuff. That's how I'm going to get paid. And incidentally, if you suck up some of my time and they start walking out because they've already seen Norm Schwartzkopf and they don't need to see any more of them, you're going to get your 75,000, but I'm not going to get mine, right? That's the way this works. This is a giant Tupperware party. And the other way to think about it is this is SeaWorld. You're Shemu the whale. You got him in the park. All the money's made at the Pearl stand, the souvenir stand, the popcorn, that's me. He laughed, you know, he thought it was fine. And so for two years, he finished one minute ahead of schedule every single time. Marches comes off the stage, salutes me as he's going off. It's very funny. He got it. Now, Colin Powell couldn't finish on time to save his life, by the way, but that was another deal altogether. So the way this deal worked is I'm on last. 25,000 people started the day. By the time I'm the last famous person's going off and I'm coming on, we're probably down to 10,000. 8,000 leave immediately. So you don't want to be standing in a door. They're leaving, right? And I'm doing my best to keep the ones I can keep because I'm in full boat because I'm only going to sell the ones that are there. So there'll be 2,000-3,000 people left. I'll sell 300 units, 400 units. Have a good day. So think about this. We started the day with 25,000. By the time we got to George Borscher, Colin Powell, or Margaret Thatcher, or whoever the last shimu was, we were down to 10. Now by the time I actually get to start selling, we're down to 2. By the time I finish, I'm probably down to 1,500. All the rest of them never heard my pitch, but they were a well-qualified audience. They didn't stay for all sorts of reasons. They're tired for one thing. They've got to get home, babysitter, husband, you know whatever. So the only guy did this, of all the speakers. I sent a sales letter afterwards to everybody who had bought tickets. Now they came in clumps, so that didn't give me 25,000 names. That got me about 10,000 names. I sent a sales letter to everybody who bought a ticket and didn't buy my stuff. So take 400 out of 10,000. I got 9,600 left to mail, which basically said, you may or may not have stayed to see me speak, but you didn't buy my stuff. In all probability, it's because you didn't stay. Because you had a babysitter, you had somebody with you who wanted to leave because you came in a group, yada, yada, yada. And so, you want to tell you what you missed, and for seven days, give you the same opportunity everybody had who stayed to the bitter end. The rest of the sales letter was a transcription of my 75-minute presentation. All I did is take the recording, transcribe it, tweak it a little bit here or there, put a subhead in every so often. But it was the pitch done as a sales letter. Always at least broke even, which is fine because we're acquiring customers. In some cases, we made money. In total customer value, probably in nine years doing that was worth 3, 3.5 mil. Now nobody else did it, even when I showed it to them, incidentally. But my point is, you take the speech, you can make a sales letter out of it. If the speech makes a sales letter, the sales letter makes a speech. So the same skill that you need to write decent copy for a sales letter is the same skill, and it's the same structure, and it's the same formula, and it's the same stories for a good audio presentation, a good video presentation, or a good speech or group seminar presentation. It's the same. You're building a sales presentation from beginning to end. They will muck that up. They won't do it if they're left to their own devices, or they go get a speech coach, or they get somebody from God forbid, Toastmasters to work with them, or they hire somebody from the local comedy club to help them. What they're going to get is a speech with a crappy commercial stuck on the end. That's what you tell them. When you suggest to them, you should be doing a group seminar to sell your cosmetic surgery, but you better let me build the seminar for you, because if you let anybody else build it for you, you're going to have a seminar with a crappy commercial stuck on the end. If we build it, you're going to have a seminar that signs up patients at the back of the room. Now again, this is a really cool opportunity. You can usually, a lot of clients you'd be working with anyway, could do it, should do it, won't know to do it unless you suggest it to them. It's another piece of work to do for the client. It's usually easy to convince them, especially if they're going to use it over and over again as a group presentation, you should get a royalty on it. Also, speakers can be clients, because they don't know how to write sales presentations either. A speaker who decides now, I need to sell stuff. When I go out and speak, they don't know how to do that. We had three people on the Peter Lowe tour that Peter wanted to use that I fixed for money. Now they're pros, and I was a pro, so it's different. I fixed their presentation, and then I coached them up so they could deliver it. I got a nice fee for it, and I got 5% of their gross for three years. That's group presentations. I'm going to give you a sample so you have a structure. Implementation training. Here's one very common scenario. I had it very early for the first time. You go create a bunch of great stuff. We create better lead generation, better sales materials, better ads, shock and awe package, DVD, send them home with this to listen to. We create all kinds of sales tools. Now the company sales people won't use them. They didn't go get their buy-in in the first place. They're threatened by them. The franchisees won't use them. The distributors won't use them, whatever. Somebody's got to come in and essentially sell the program to the sales force, to the distributors, to the franchise owners. You want to do that, first of all, especially if you've got royalties, because you want the client to be successful with what you're creating for them. However, it's something you can also get paid for. I had it happen to me very early. I learned it by accident. I had it happen very early with a client that had a bunch of real estate offices. I get hired to redo all this advertising and marketing, and we discover as we're going along, the agents are all going to bulk at all of this. Then I get paid to run around and convince the agents that we've invented the greatest thing since sliced bread, and here's how to use it, and here's why you should use it, and so forth. As recently as my miracle era client, after I got the entire contract signed for the entire project, I said, oh, by the way, good phrase. I said, oh, by the way, right? You already know you got franchisee problems. That's one of the things that brought you to me is the franchisees complaining about your marketing. I said, but I got bad news for you. You're not going to spend all this money to fix that, and the franchisees are going to bulk, and they're not going to use it, and they're going to argue with you, and they're not going to understand why we're doing what we're doing, and then their sales people are going to bulk at it and sabotage it, and so forth. You want to come up with a plan and a strategy in order to fix that preemptively and immediately upon implementation. That's all I said. He calls, asks for a phone appointment about a week later. I get on a phone with him. He's not been thinking about what you said. They're going to ruin it all, and they're going to blame us for it, and they're not going to use it right, and you said we needed a strategy for that. What's the strategy? I said, well, you have a couple of choices. I said, you could use your corporate people to get them all together and do meetings, and blow breath on them, and show them everything, and explain to them exactly why it's being done the way it's being done, and all that, just essentially as I'm explaining it to you. I said, I imagine the problem you got with that is they all think the guys from the home office are morons, but I don't know. I said, so you could do that. I said, you could try and find a champion, one of your own guys, who falls in love with it, who'll do that with everybody else. I said, or you can hire like an outside speaker or trainer or somebody to do that. I said, or I suppose, if you really think it would be best, I guess I'd be willing to do it. So now I add on to this project, which is already a million dollar fee and a million dollar royalty project. I add on two speaking engagements, two tele-seminars, and a sales training manual that has to be built on how to use the stuff on built-in form, and that's $18,000 times three, so that's 30, that's $54,000, so that's 54 grand in additional money. Sales training manual, fortunately, happens to be one I already got for somebody else that just needs to be tweaked and changed around, and really, I needed to go do the speaking. I needed to get everybody to use it anyway, so I'm getting paid to do something I had to do, and I sold my own product at the speaking engagements from the front of the room, so the franchisees all bought my stuff, which I didn't split with the client because he didn't ask, so, well, you don't ask, you don't get. So very profitable add-on. So some version of implementation training. The second place that occurs, has occurred for me often on over the years, and will occur for you, is you will have the client who prefers or truthfully needs to do a lot of our kind of work in-house. They really, because they either need so much of it, they need it always day-to-day, they need it in a hurry, their business doesn't really justify paying top rates to freelancers to do everything. So I usually sell one of two things or two things. I will usually sell them a bank of copy that now is designed to be used again and again and again and again and again, like a Chinese menu, take one from column A, one from column C, stitch them together, it's good enough to go. So I'll create them a notebook of headlines, opening paragraphs, answers to price objections, bridges to offers, so here's your library. And now you're in-house people, could keep going in here and cutting and pasting, and it ain't going to be spectacular, but it's going to be infinitely better than they would do on their own, and you can come back to me in five years and we'll do it all again all over again, right? So I've done that a number of times. Then either with that or instead of that, okay, I'll come in and I'll train your copywriters. And we'll do the best we can. Bring me in for three days and we'll workshop them. So I'll teach them 10 fundamentals of direct response, and then we'll take your stuff, which is crappy, and together we'll fix it so they have the experience of brainstorming an offer and brainstorming a guarantee and all that, and then the third day we'll go through it all again, and by the time we're done, they'll be a lot better than they are now, right? And you can put your writers in there, you can put some of your marketing executives in there who don't get it, you can put your sister along in there if you want to, who every time you show her your stuff, that is good, she screams and yells about how ugly and unprofessional it is, you can put anybody in there you want, right? And so like Peter Codd, he sold out, but he owned a software company, they invented, so this is real eggheading, they invented C++ software, which a lot, so you know what that is. I wrote copy for it, I don't know what it is. But, so Peter brought me in to teach his guys what direct response is, so I spent three days teaching what direct response is. An ad agency in Houston, a guy who met on an airplane, owns an ad agency in Houston, I'm flying, I don't know where I was flying to, but we're in first class talking, what do you do? And he said, you know, I own an ad agency, I said, oh, you do regular advertising, right? It kind of doesn't work. And so we had the whole Ogilvy conversation, you know, and he said, well he said, to tell you the truth, he said, you know, there's nobody in our agency who knows squat about direct response. He says, you want to come talk to him about it? I said, I'll be happy to come talk to him about it, so I get booked to go, teaches ad agency guys direct response. I later got a nice client from a back door. So implementation training basically falls into two places, one, teaching people how to use what it is you're creating for them so they don't muck it all up after you give it to them. Okay, number two is as a replacement for doing work for them. So they actually do want their people taught. I'll tell you another client. So trim spa, this came from Halbert too. Alex Gowan, he owned trim spa, if you don't remember trim spa, had a four year huge run-up did very great. And then he had a co-smith as a spokesperson. They had a locker in a room to keep the weight off, but that's neither here nor there. And they were in Walmart stores everywhere and so forth. And he also owns a company that does the $29 lose weight stop smoking hypnosis seminars. You'll see the ads and newspapers and you just show up at the hotel, you pay 29 bucks and they woo you and you lose weight and you don't smoke. So Alex had decided to go in house. So he had used Halbert, he had used other copywriters and he said, you know, we just need so much stuff, I need to tweak it all the time. It's cost, you know, the copywriters are making as much money as I'm making and I don't want to write the stuff myself. I got four or five people who were like, okay, but they could be better. I got a couple of people I think I want to keep who are executives, but they're totally resistant to direct marketing. How about you coming in and giving them the talk? You know, okay, fine, I'll come in and give them the talk. So I spent a day there giving them the talk. I'll tell you something, I'll tell you something great. I spent a day there giving them the talk and then I spent a day working with the two copywriters, essentially workshopping, sitting at a big table with all their stuff, critiquing and teaching them the 12 boxes. Right? Here's how you might fix this, here's how you might fix that. Nice feat. So before I get there, Alex pretty tough. Alex made everybody read the ultimate sales letter and the ultimate marketing plan. So everybody now files in the room. So before he says, before we start the meeting, I just want to make sure everybody's prepared so we're going to have a little quiz. And he proceeds to ask them questions on the books, right? So one guy confesses, after he doesn't know two answers, that he hasn't read the books. And Alex says, well, you're fired. Now, do you know the answer to, fired them on the spot, on the spot? I will tell you, I had everybody's attention for the rest of the day. So that's implementation training. Referral commissions and markups. All right? Two basic premises here. We've talked about both of them, but I want to make the point again. So premise number one is, you should get paid for every value you bring. So if I know where to get that whiz-bang envelope or that stuffed poo poo poo, and we need to use the stuffed poo poo poo for their campaign, or I know about his birthday campaign, and I'm going to bring that value to the table, I should get paid. If I'm going to hook them up with somebody, and it's going to be mutually beneficial, I should get paid. But certainly on every vendor situation, I should get paid. So A, you want to disclose it. I showed it to you in my agreements. This is the way things work around here. And B, you want to make sure you get paid. So you either get paid from the vendor, or you get paid by bundling and marking up one or the other. But you should get paid. So and often it's small money, but small money adds up. And so that's the first thing. The second thing is you should bring as much value to the table as you can. So turning clients loose to do pretty much anything on their own is a bad idea. A, they may fall into the wrong hands. B, they may screw it up. C, you may lose control over the client altogether to that vendor, and everybody he has a social association with. So you want to keep control of as much as possible that has any relationship whatsoever to the project or projects that you are working on. So you want dependence on you. You want to be the go-to person, and you want control over the client. The second reason it's a bad idea is because if you ask the client to do much in order to get something implemented that is either complicated, difficult, confusing, requires them to go find somebody, deal with somebody, there's a reasonably good chance they won't. And so now your work doesn't get implemented, or it gets implemented half-assed. So getting the stuffed cacochcas was a big damn problem. So at the last minute we just decided to stick in a photograph of a stuffed cacochca. Well, but we're supposed to have stuffed cacochcas because it's lumpy and it goes in a box and it doesn't go in an envelope. Well, it's the same. No, it's not the same. Right? It's supposed to be an oversized, full-color Tyvek envelope with a window that a fake check shows through and it's got to be on real check paper. Well, getting real check paper was a pain in the ass. And getting it individually addressed by hand was a pain in the ass. And my printer couldn't print full-color on Tyvek. So it went out in a regular paper envelope with no window and a fake check inside that's printed on goldrod paper. Well, well, it's the same. No, it's not the same. And I'm going to tell you, they're going to do it to you all the time, endlessly. So the more control you take of the whole thing, better for the client, happier client, better for you, okay, because he won't muck it up. So you take something as simple as a silver platter, okay? So it's on his thing over there. I have a client, by the way. I'll tell you a silver platter story real quick. I have a client whose name is Jay Geyer. He sells very elaborate, fixed-the-incoming call services to professional practices mostly, dentist, doctor, lawyer, et cetera. So Jay, about a few years ago, he and his wife built this huge, beautiful new home in a Ritzy suburb of Atlanta. And when you walk into Forer, there's a circular staircase and there's a chandelier, and nailed to the wall is one of them crappy silver platters. And next to it is my picture, stuck up there with a tack in the middle of this thing. And the reason it's there is because my silver platter letter bought the house, and they think it's cool and they tell the story to everybody. And so those things make a difference. But I have a client, honest to God, who I turned them loose, built the whole campaign for them. Step two, the thing's supposed to go on the silver platter in a box. Here's what he sent. A picture of the silver platter stapled to the letter. That's what he sent. Because like a moron, I didn't deal with getting them the silver platter and controlling the whole process. So for all those reasons, you want to be in control and you want to get paid. And all these little monies can add up. So for example, with Miracle Air, so initially I paid Chris Mullins, she's in your resource directory, to do mystery phone shopping at the stores out of my pocket because I needed to know certain things. Then with the evidence that there was a serious problem here, I said, you got to pay. She's got to do your stores. Now they're franchise, so the corporate, so the usual fight of who's good. So we never got system-wide use. But for a goodly period of time, we had, I don't know, maybe a third of the franchise owners using her service every month from which I got an override check. And in a period of time, it totaled to about $27,000. It's not a lot of money, but it's $27,000 I didn't have to do any work for other than say you got a user, here's a phone number and send the facts to Chris and say chase these people because they need you and I need you to be helping them make this happen and be sure to send me my check because I got horses that want to eat. So again, over a two and a half year period, $27,000 is not a lot of money, but you get $27,000 here, you get four here, you get $600,000 here. Before you know it, you've made some, a mortgage payments and they didn't pay any more than they would have anyway. I brought the value to the table. I'm entitled to the money. I even spent a little money out of my pocket to grease the skids in the first place to make it happen. So that's the position you ought to take about all of this. The last part of this conversation, we'll look at the samples very quickly. Page 218, if you're following along in a song book, is equity, not just income. So the worst thing about copyrighting business, consulting business, any professional service business is it's really more of a job than it is a business because it's dependent on us working in order to get money and our work feeds the present bank, but it doesn't do a very good job of feeding the future bank and it does almost nothing to feed the permanent bank. So while our client is stacking up Panera Bread Stores that are eventually going to be sold for a nice big chunk of change, we are getting fees and royalties, but we're not going to get a big chunk of change when the Panera Bread Stores sell even though we are largely responsible for the fact that the Panera Bread Stores are going to sell for $20 million instead of for $2 million. So this is not good. In many cases it's unavoidable, but it is still something to have angst over. So there are a couple of ways to think about this. So one is that you can trade fees or royalties for equity in businesses. People do it with startups, for example. They do it with spin-offs from a business. The bad news about that is, there's two pieces of bad news. One is you also get a lot of liability because when you own part of a business, even a tiny little part, you're on the hook with the guy who owns the big part. And even worse, if the guy who owns the big part comes up empty, you might be last man standing and you might find yourself responsible for a lot of problems. So I'm not a big fan of having equity in other people's businesses I don't control for that reason. The second reason is, being a minority stockholder in a privately held company and trying to get equity out of it is not the easiest thing on the planet because they could keep inflating their salaries, they can decide to buy a private jet, the guy can put his 17 mistresses on the payroll, which is shocking, that never occurred to Tiger. Well, at least it would have been pre-tax dollars. Trying to get your money is not easy. So I have an alternative formula that I use. So I'll share that with you. My alternative formula is what I call an equity kicker. So my alternative formula is fees, royalties, whatever the present bank compensation is, but an agreed upon amount of money or percentage if and when the company is sold or taken public. So I don't own any equity, but I'm going to get a piece of the equity when the equity changes hands. So that's my alternative formula. And right now I have this in about eight situations. I've had it mature and pay off in about three and I've had it turn into a mess and gotten screwed out of it in one. So not bad. You know, it's a ratio. I mean, I'll take that. So that's the alternative formula for that. The second way to think about this, which I've mentioned a number of times, is to have your work create a owned or jointly owned with client asset that can then be licensed, franchised, syndicated, resold somehow, again and again and again to others. By the way, you will notice if you go back when you study my agreements, you will see language that was added. So I think I gave you a 2002 example and I gave you a current example. You will see language in the current example that prohibits them from reselling the work product I am giving them. So there's language in there that says it's for use only in your own business and you can't resell it, publish it, license it, syndicate it, etc. That got added after somebody did it. So I got paid 50 grand to write a whole system which he then turned around and was selling for $20,000 to empty numbers of people. Now I can't be grumpy at them because I didn't tell them he couldn't, but I only had to see it happen once to decide to tell everybody they couldn't. If we're going to resell it by God, we're going to resell it together. So a real cool thing, for example, the shock and awe. So this project. So this was done for Craig Proctor and at the time, Craig's one of the top remax agents in the world with his own real estate practice in Toronto, Canada, but he's also a coach to the real estate profession, like wherever you are, your person is to the dental profession. And so at the top end of his information marketing pyramid is a platinum level coaching program of top agents, million dollar producers who pay to be in a group, be with them three times a year, etc. And so in my regular consulting call coming up with Craig, we really talked about two things. So he spent half the call complaining about this new thing happening in real estate where he used to get gimmies because he's the most famous, biggest, best, etc. in his area. And now everybody before they hire an agent, they have eight agents come over on eight different nights and everybody does their dog and pony show and then they pick the one they like best and it could be Margie who sold one house last year, but she brought cookies and the dog liked her so boom, she gets it. So he spends the first half of the call telling me about that. And the second half talking about his coaching business and how renewal time is coming up with all these platinum guys and he wants them to give them another 25,000 bucks and what is he good to do to juice the renewal? And I said, I got a solution to both things. I said, A, let's build something to fix problem A that you don't like, which all your guys have the same problem because they're all the top producers in their area. So if you're having to deal with Margie and the cookies, they're having to deal with Margie and the cookies. So let's build something to fix it for you and let's make the extra benefit now, the new benefit that the platinum members get is every year you and I do a project for your business and they get to use it and they get to watch it happen and they get to see it tested and they get to see the bugs worked out and then they get a license right to use it, which ultimately because it'll help you sell platinum and if you want you could raise the price of platinum a little bit if you wanted to. Now it'll make all the money you got to pay me to do it nearly free. So this was the first of three things we did over three years. So I got my fee to create it for him and I got X number of dollars per platinum member who joined renewed or joined platinum and a bonus for every one of them who implemented it and I did three coaching calls in addition to doing the work implementation calls to try and convince him to implement it. So that's a little piece of leverage, a different way to use a client. So there's this creating an asset with the client that you and the client agree one way or another, you are going to turn into a profitable thing on its own. The third way to create equity and it's the least of them but it is still important to keep in mind is at bare minimum as you do work you want to create templates that you are going to be able to use again and again and again and again. You don't want to be reinventing everything from scratch for every client. It's too slow. So I was very happy when Miracle Air paid me to create that particular version of the shock and awe package because all the while I was doing it I was templating it at the same time knowing, man I'm going to be able to use this again and again and again and again, fitting client after client after client in it and it's going to save me a ton of time. I'm going to charge the same amount of money every time I do it but it's going to save me a ton of time. So every one of them is going to have a who loves whatever piece in it and everyone is going to have a diagram, the process piece in it. So at the same time I'm building it for client A, I'm building it as a fill in the blank template that I'm going to be able to use again and again and again and again. So now I kind of figured that out really totally about ten years ago. So I have about thirty of those that are like off the shelf ready to go, very elaborate templates for campaigns, projects, component parts that really are pretty much fill in the blank. And so I'm billing out eight, nine, ten, twelve days worth of work and I'm really doing two. Now that's not really equity but it is because these are assets that have recyclable value and it's at least leverage. So in your sample kit now, here's what I gave you so you know what you got. Mostly I'm going to let you take it home and study it but I'll point a couple things out to you. You don't have page numbers so I'm going to show you the first one of each chunk and then you're going to have to go hunt. So the first thing you should see in there says webinar structure at the top. So again, whether you do it the first time for a client or not, you want to develop a structure that you're going to live with again and again and again and again. So this happens to be a webinar selling one of my products for a host, for a joint venture partner who has real estate investors as clients. But I had to write it so it didn't... So the way this one was built first of all so you know is he's been doing tell seminars every month with different guests, selling different things to his subscribers, to his customers. So he's been doing that for three years. So I took the 41 most successful, had them all transcribed, went through and identified the commonalities that were in most or all of them and then built my structure from the 41 previous successful tell seminars that had occurred from all these other people. And then I stuck my content into that structure. So that's how this was built. Most people don't do the homework by the way to get it right. So this structure and I made handwritten notes about what everything is. So you not only see the outline of the content but you see the actual structure. So that first page takes you through the structure, everything that's here and then I gave you the script of this one. So you have the word for word script of this one exactly as it occurred now within this structure. And I made notes about what each thing is. So you could build webinar or tell a seminar right off of that structure for just about anything. The next thing you want to look for is a bunch of pages that look like this. They have a panel down the side for notes and a renegade millionaire logo at the bottom. Okay, find it. Okay. So this is a transcript and then I have made side notes for you. So the side notes were all done for you. This is a transcript of the speech I used to sell magnetic marketing for some 12 years both at the big success events and elsewhere. So this is a transcript of that speech. It's about a $20 million speech because that's how much stuff it sold from the front of the room. I happen to think it's the best piece of copywriting I've ever done. Certainly in terms of money it's probably the best piece of copywriting I've ever done. So there's a speech that sells script. And again on the side I've made notes for you about what's there, why it's there, and so forth. So from this with the side notes you could pull a structure just like I did for the webinar structure into which you could fit a pitch for just about anybody selling anything in a speech form. So you have that whole script. Next you want to look for a page so when those pages stop that don't have that sidebar on them anymore you will find an ugly little page that looks like this. It starts with making a lot of money in and of itself as a pretty easy thing to figure out how to do. The handwritten note at the right is anchor. This is the opening monologue for a discovery day that sells a franchise. A non-franchise but for our conversation it doesn't matter. So this is the general setup opening monologue at a discovery day when candidates who might buy a franchise, this particular franchise have all come to decide whether or not they're going to buy it. So this is the opening monologue for that that sets up the specific sale. This is a specific one but it has been cloned dozens and dozens of times. I gave you the script and if you keep going into the pages I gave you the overheads that match the script and they're numbered so you can match them up with the script. As an aside the discovery day format can be used for all sorts of things. It could be used to sell people on to cruise ships and sell them cruises. It could be used to sell them in anything pricey enough to justify bringing them to one place to look at it. The format is an opening monologue that makes the case generally for why what they're doing already is no good and why they need to do this. It sets up the specific presentation. The day ends with everybody either in or out. If they're out they go home, if they're in they go to a celebratory dinner and they stay for a second day of some kind of implementation. Fast start training, book their cruise, whatever. So that's the opening monologue for a discovery day. If you get to the end of that which will be the end of the overheads then you will find a page that looks like this. It's headlined Amazing America hosted by Alan Thick. This is an audio cassette script for a multi-level marketing company in the nutrition field. It's one of its top distributors. The project here, by the way, again taking control of everything. I wrote the script. I had to interview. You'll see there's a bunch of his distributors who are doctors interviewed all throughout the tape. I had to pre-interview everybody who was going to be at the recording session, transcribe the pre-interview, turn it into talking points for them to use to control what they said and the time allotted that they said. Write the script. I had to go get him Alan Thick and I had to book the recording studio, get everybody together for the recording studio, be there, pick Alan up at the airport and make Alan happy on the way to the recording studio and then supervise the recording and then take Alan back to the airport, supervise the editing of the recording and deliver a finished master. Now the copywriting is the most important part of that whole process but you turn them loose to do the rest of it and it's like over. Plus, there's a lot of money. There's money on the Alan Thick fee from the celebrity broker. There's money from the recording studio. There's money from the editor and then because my vendor presses the first run of the product, there's money from the product. Plus the copywriting fee and the royalty which doesn't sound like much, it was two cents of tape but there were like two and a half million copies of this tape put out there. So it adds up. And this is a, you can pull from this if you want, structure but really this one is more about you seeing the idea of that kind of a project. If you get to the end of it, you will see a page that looks like this, Dan Kennedy's Practice Promotion Seminar Presentation. So this is a script from a three hour evening preview seminar selling doctors a practice building system. This, I don't know, probably produced $10, $12 million over two years and it migrated from the group presentation live to a recorded presentation that was sent with a sales letter in the mail to make the same sale. So you have actually the script that migrated. You have the audio cassette or the audio CD version of the seminar but my portions of the seminar, what you see as Dan in the script were live recorded at the seminar and then cut apart and re-edited and a narrator stuck in. Now from this, you could draw again structure and I have many times. So every time I create a group presentation that somebody is going to do in a room to sell things, I go right back to this. I build off of this. So you have that as I think, no, there's one more example. So if you get to the end of that, you will find a page that looks like this, make a $3,000 profit from a $399 investment. So this is the script of an audio for you to use. So when I got them as a client, their business consisted of running a magazine ad, getting leads, sending them a product brochure and an order form for the kits of the stuff that they would then take out and sell and make money with. So the first thing I added was a sales letter because they were sending them a brochure with pretty pictures and an order form. The guys got to figure out kind of for himself, just from what was in the ad, I buy this stuff, I put it in my car, I go to a swap meet, I set up a swap meet, people come out, give me money, I make money. You got to do all that in his own head. I said to land. So the first thing I got paid to do was a sales letter. Then when that, to his shock and surprise, improved results from what he was sending out to the leads, then we built an audio presentation to go in with the sales letter. So that's what this is. Then the results were so good, now working the leads that we went on TV and I got to do an infomercial form, which ran for eight years, still holds the record as a lead generation show, longest running in infomercial history. So this audio thing that you have in your hands, just so you know, so I had to choose the testimonials out of the available testimonials. I had to pre-interview them, that I had to create each person's talking points that they were actually going to say when we recorded them. I scripted the client portion word for word and then I supervised the recording in the production of the audio cassette. So that's your last sample in your sample kit. Left in your notebook, just so you know, you should have a section that looks like this. And so these are some resources and vendors for you. There also should be, I must have skipped over it. What page was that, because you asked me about it, what page is the influential writing thing on? I would talk about it again, 190-ish. 190-ish? That's helpful. So on 190-ish, okay, let's go there first. So 192, you'll find a sales letter, first of all, it's a really good sales letter, it worked really well, so you might want to pay attention to it. But beyond that, this is one of those few resources I told you I was going to mention to you specifically. So this is, of course, a set of resources. You might want to get your little pause on. You can take a look at it on a break and stuff and while we do Q&A. But it is my whole seminar on influential writing, which is not really direct response writing so much as it is the motorcycle philosophy kind of book, the newsletter, the glue, the bonding with the customer, the maintaining the relationship with the customer, the making yourself a celebrity, making yourself famous. So I won't belabor the point, I'll just point out to you that there's a discount offer on it that we just made to our people at our super conference. I'll extend the same to you, but you do have to turn the orders in here. So you've got to rip the last page out, fill out the order form and turn it in while you're here. If you want that offer, please do not send it in after the fact, expecting to get that offer because you will not. Now back to this section, which is page 220. So here you have a little collection of resources. Folks I rely on, companies I rely on, sort of my back office for a lot of the work that I do, including folks that were here and folks that aren't here. You are welcome to use my name if you should wish to contact them.
